Title: Tate brothers to fight 'politically motivated' UK extradition
Article:
MIAMI: Influencer Andrew Tate and his brother Tristan will fight extradition to Britain on sex crime charges, their lawyer said Monday, slamming their recent arrest in Miami as "politically motivated."
US authorities detained the self-professed misogynist and his younger sibling on Saturday as UK prosecutors announced new charges of rape, sex trafficking and assault.
The Tate brothers now face 59 charges in total – 42 against Andrew and 17 against Tristan.
"We are, of course, objecting to extradition because Andrew and Tristan are innocent. They've never done nothing wrong. They shouldn't be extradited for crimes they did not commit," lawyer Joseph McBride said.
"It's politically motivated. There's no question about it," he told reporters outside a Miami court following a hearing on the case.
McBride said their arrest came days after the brothers traveled to Washington to meet with members of Congress.
"They walked the grounds of the Capitol, and three days later, they're in the jumpsuit. You do the math," he said.
Andrew Tate is one of the most prominent proponents of the so-called "manosphere" network of communities, many of them online, that focus on traditional masculinity, anti-feminism, and self-improvement.
He promotes his divisive views, often incorporating alpha-male and aggressively misogynistic themes, to millions on social media including 10.8 million followers on X.
In Romania, where they have been based in recent years, the Tates face allegations of trafficking minors, sexual intercourse with a minor and money laundering.
In March 2025, the US state of Florida launched a criminal investigation against the brothers. The status of that investigation is unclear.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Economists: Diesel subsidy reform is working, focus now on the savings [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's targeted diesel subsidy reform is beginning to generate the fiscal savings policymakers had hoped for, but economists say the government's next challenge is ensuring those savings are put to productive use.
While the mechanism is expected to gradually curb subsidy leakages and normalise diesel consumption over the coming months, they said the bigger test of the reform will be whether it creates lasting economic benefits through better public spending.
Universiti Teknologi Malaysia associate professor in property economics Dr Muhammad Najib Razali said the reform marked a shift away from blanket fuel subsidies, which are inherently prone to leakages.
"This is important because blanket fuel subsidies are structurally vulnerable to leakage.
"When diesel is sold below market value, it creates incentives for overconsumption, false claims, industrial misuse, and smuggling, especially when domestic prices are lower than prices in neighbouring markets," he told Business Times.
Najib said targeted eligibility, fleet card controls and direct assistance allow the government to better direct support towards those who genuinely need it, while reducing the number of unintended beneficiaries.
The government currently provides subsidised diesel at RM2.10 per litre for eligible recipients under its targeted subsidy programme, while the unsubsidised retail price stands at RM4.37 per litre in Peninsular Malaysia.
The programme uses a MyKad-based verification mechanism, similar to the approach adopted under the Budi Madani Ron95 initiative.
Najib said early indications suggest the fiscal savings from the reform are significant and could even exceed the government's initial expectations.
Malaysia's diesel subsidy bill surged from RM1.4 billion in 2019 to RM14.3 billion in 2023 before the targeted subsidy programme was introduced.
The government initially estimated annual savings of about RM4 billion from the reform, although more recent figures indicate savings have reached around RM5 billion.
"More recent public figures indicate that the government has saved about RM5 billion through diesel subsidy rationalisation.
"From a fiscal perspective, this is meaningful because subsidy savings create fiscal space for more productive public expenditure," he said.
Najib said the more important question is not whether the government spends less on subsidies, but whether the savings are redirected to areas that generate greater social and economic returns.
These include public transport, rural roads, logistics infrastructure, flood mitigation, healthcare, education and targeted cash assistance.
"Therefore, the reform should be understood as a fiscal reallocation strategy rather than only a cost-cutting measure.
"If the government can demonstrate that diesel subsidy savings are being redirected to visible infrastructure and social protection programmes, public acceptance of subsidy rationalisation is likely to improve," he said.
Meanwhile, OCBC senior Asean economist Lavanya Venkateswaran said it is still too early to determine the full fiscal impact of the reform, although lower global diesel prices have coincided with adjustments to the Budi Diesel mechanism.
"Further increases in the subsidy bill will be limited as a result," she said in an email reply.
Lavanya said Malaysia's sustained fiscal consolidation and proactive debt management also strengthen the case for further sovereign credit rating upgrades, supported by solid economic growth and sound policy management.
Malaysia is rated A- by S&P Global Ratings and A3 by Moody's Ratings, while Fitch Ratings assigns the country a BBB+ rating. All three agencies maintain a stable outlook.
She said while the targeted diesel subsidy mechanism has begun to achieve its intended objectives, it will take time for its full impact on subsidy leakages to become evident.
"We do expect the change in the diesel mechanism to normalise diesel consumption in the coming months," she said.
FVDXmitFn-0
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Economists: Malaysia's trade surge reflects real demand, not just base effect
Article:
Azanis Shahila Aman
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's sharp rise in trade in June was not merely the result of a favourable base effect, but also reflected genuine strength in the country's external sector, economists said.
Total trade jumped 44.7 per cent year-on-year to RM340.9 billion, with exports rising 45.4 per cent to RM177.9 billion and imports increasing 43.9 per cent to RM163 billion.
The performance extended Malaysia's trade surplus streak to 74 consecutive months, with the surplus expanding 64.9 per cent to RM14.9 billion.
Economists attributed the strong showing to sustained demand for electrical and electronics (E&E) products, energy exports and commodities.
They said resilient global technology demand, together with continued investment in artificial intelligence (AI), cloud computing and data centres, had positioned Malaysia to benefit from the ongoing recovery in global trade.
However, they said the exceptionally strong headline growth was partly amplified by last year's lower base and was likely to moderate in the second half amid geopolitical tensions, tariff uncertainty and uneven growth across major markets.
MORE THAN A LOW-BASE EFFECT
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said the latest trade figures reflect genuine improvements in Malaysia's export performance rather than merely statistical distortions arising from a weaker base last year.
He said Malaysia continues to benefit from strong external demand, particularly for E&E products, liquefied natural gas (LNG) and crude oil.
"When it comes to AI and cloud computing, people associate them with data centre investments and semiconductor-related exports. In this respect, Malaysia's presence is quite material," he told the Business Times.
The strength was evident in E&E exports, which recorded a twelfth consecutive month of double-digit growth, rising 56.9 per cent in June after increasing 70 per cent in May.
LNG exports also surged 83.3 per cent, while crude oil exports rebounded 112.4 per cent after contracting in the previous two months.
Afzanizam noted that exports grew 42.4 per cent in the second quarter of 2026, up from 12.6 per cent in the first quarter, while import growth accelerated to 25.4 per cent from 7.6 per cent.
The stronger trade performance lifted Malaysia's quarterly trade surplus to RM84 billion from RM63.2 billion in the preceding quarter.
"This could mean Malaysia's current account surplus will widen in the second quarter, which would be positive for the ringgit. In a nutshell, the improvement was real," he said.
GLOBAL DEMAND PROVING MORE RESILIENT
Economist Dr Geoffrey Williams said the latest trade data suggests global demand has remained stronger than expected despite geopolitical tensions and the conflict in the Middle East.
He said exports to the United States remained particularly encouraging.
"Although shipments to the US eased slightly on a month-on-month basis, exports still recorded an impressive 108.6 per cent increase from a year earlier.
"This is further proof that concerns surrounding US President Donald Trump's tariffs were misplaced and that trade with the US remains very positive," he said.
Williams added that although Malaysia's monthly trade surplus narrowed from May, the broader trend remained firmly positive.
"The trade balance has fallen significantly month on month but is still up 64.9 per cent for the year to June. This continues a strong recovery in the trade balance over the past 12 months," he said.
He added that higher oil prices linked to geopolitical tensions have yet to significantly disrupt global trade.
"Global uncertainties have not affected markets as much as expected. Although oil prices have edged higher in recent days, they remain well below earlier peaks," he said.
GROWTH EXPECTED TO MODERATE
Universiti Teknologi Mara Faculty of Business and Management senior lecturer Dr Mohamad Idham Md Razak said June's strong trade performance reflected both a genuine recovery in demand and favourable base effects.
"A significant portion of the surge can be attributed to a low base effect, especially if the same period last year was marked by weaker trade activity or external shocks.
"However, there are also signs of a recovery in global demand, particularly for electronics, commodities and intermediate goods, as supply chains continue to normalise," he said.
Idham cautioned that while the June figures were encouraging, the headline growth may overstate the pace of the underlying recovery.
"In reality, the underlying momentum is likely to be more moderate once adjusted for base effects," he said.
EXTERNAL RISKS REMAIN A KEY CHALLENGE
Despite the strong first-half performance, Idham expects Malaysia's trade momentum to moderate in the second half as exporters navigate an increasingly uncertain external environment.
He said the recovery in global demand should continue gradually, supported by stabilising economic conditions in major markets and improvements in the global technology cycle.
However, geopolitical tensions, tariff uncertainty and uneven growth among Malaysia's key trading partners remain significant downside risks.
"As a result, export growth is likely to moderate from the exceptionally strong June pace, while imports should continue to be supported by resilient domestic demand and investment activity," he said.
Overall, Idham expects Malaysia's trade performance to remain resilient, although growth is likely to become more measured as external headwinds persist.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 4 US service members killed in deadliest Iran-linked attacks on US forces
Article:
RIYADH: Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis said on Monday they would impose a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, opening a potential new front against the United States in its war with Iran and raising the threat to global energy supplies and trade beyond the Gulf.
The escalation comes after one of the deadliest periods in the war for US troops. The Pentagon on Monday identified two US soldiers who were killed when Iran attacked an American base in Jordan on Friday, while they also said they had found unidentified remains that could be those of a third soldier reported as missing in action.
Separately, a fourth service member was killed in northern Iraq during the "controlled detonation of unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian one-way attack drone."
After the Houthis announced their blockade, the Saudi-led coalition in Yemen said in a statement that it would respond to them with force, and also that it began implementing protection measures for its ships moving through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, a key export route for Saudi oil after the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
The Houthis made their announcement despite signs that Teheran and Washington want to resume diplomacy to halt a worsening cycle of attacks that has all but wrecked a fragile interim agreement signed last month. Iran's Foreign Ministry said mediators had presented "proposals" to Teheran, signalling that diplomatic contacts remain active, but gave no details.
Oil prices rose briefly after the Houthi statement but later fell back as traders held out hope of a diplomatic breakthrough. The insurance cost of shipping goods through the Red Sea rose on the increased risks for merchant shipping.
Iran had been pressing the Houthis to close the Bab el-Mandeb gateway to the Red Sea if the US continued to attack Iranian power infrastructure.
A full closure of the strait would reduce  global oil supply by 7pct as it would leave most of Saudi oil  exports unable to leave the region. The disruption would add to the huge cut to oil flows from the war in the Gulf,  which has already reduced shipments by 10pct of global supply.
In their statement, the Houthis' armed forces said they were declaring "a maritime embargo against the criminal Saudi enemy, based on the equation of 'an eye for an eye', effective immediately" in response to what they called "an unjust and oppressive siege" imposed on Yemen by the Saudis.
DIPLOMATIC PUSH TO RESTORE CEASEFIRE
A senior Iranian official told Reuters on Monday that Teheran had received a proposal from mediators for a 10-day ceasefire in efforts to salvage the interim deal, intended to pave the way to a lasting agreement to end the war that began on Feb 28 with US-Israeli attacks on Iran.
Neither the ministry nor the official who spoke to Reuters gave any details on the putative ceasefire discussions.
Two Pakistani government sources said separately that Iranian Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni had asked Pakistan to resume its mediation role in the conflict. He later arrived in Islamabad for further talks.
The diplomatic push followed another night of US strikes on Iranian cities and attacks by Iran's Revolutionary Guards on US military assets across the region. US Central Command said Monday afternoon US time that it had begun another round of strikes on Iran.
President Donald Trump, under mounting political pressure at home over rising gasoline prices since the war began and Iran effectively closed the vital Strait of Hormuz, defended the latest strikes on Iran as honouring the deaths of American service members killed in recent Iranian attacks.
"Every time Iran kills an American Soldier they will pay for that killing many times over! This directive has been passed on to Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, Daniel Caine, and every Leader in the Military," Trump wrote on Truth Social on Monday.
The war has killed thousands of people, mostly in Iran and Lebanon, part of which has been occupied by Israeli forces following attacks on Israel by Hizbollah fighters who said they were acting in solidarity with Teheran. Lebanese President Joseph Aoun is due to meet Trump at the White House on Tuesday to present a plan on how to disarm Iran-aligned Hizbollah and secure an Israeli withdrawal.
Israel also still has forces in Gaza following two years of war ignited by the Palestinian Hamas' attack on southern Israeli communities on Oct 7, 2023. Hamas said on Monday it had named top negotiator Khalil Al-Hayya as its overall leader, a move that an analyst said could signal a tougher stance by the group towards calls for it to disarm.
IRAN SAYS TANKERS EXPLODED
The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said two oil tankers had exploded after attempting to transit the strait via an "unsafe" route. On Sunday, the IRGC had said two ships were involved in an "accident" in the same area. It was unclear whether the two incidents were related.
Reuters could not immediately verify the incident. The IRGC statement gave no details about the vessels or any casualties.
Separately, a vessel was struck by an unknown projectile off Oman's coast, overlooking the Strait of Hormuz, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency said.
In Iran, explosions were reported in Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini. One person was killed and several wounded southwest of Tabriz, according to state news agency IRNA.
The IRGC said it targeted US aircraft at Jordan's Aqaba airport with ballistic missiles, as well as military assets at Kuwait's Al-Adiri camp and Ali Al Salem Air Base, and positions in Syria. Sirens sounded in Bahrain throughout Monday. Kuwait's army said early Tuesday that air defences were again intercepting Iranian drones.--REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SpaceX to report first earnings as public company on Aug 4
Article:
NEW YORK: SpaceX will release its first quarterly earnings report as a publicly traded company on Aug 4, the rocket and artificial intelligence (AI) giant said Monday on its investor relations website.
The Elon Musk-led company, which trades under the ticker SPCX on the Nasdaq exchange, is scheduled to report results after markets close that day, at 2130 GMT. The figures will cover the second quarter, which ended June 30.
The announcement comes as shares in the company have fallen well below their initial public offering price.
SpaceX went public on June 12 at US$135 a share, with the stock later soaring as high as US$225.64 and pushing the company's valuation above US$2.6 trillion.
Shares have since tumbled, closing at US$119.85 on Monday -- more than 45 per cent below their post-initial public offering (IPO) peak.
The company, which reported a loss of US$4.3 billion last year in its IPO filings, has a long-term goal of developing data centers in space. Analysts do not expect profitability anytime soon.
SpaceX now operates three divisions: a launch and space unit, the Starlink satellite internet service, and an artificial intelligence arm that includes the Grok chatbot and social media platform X.
Starlink, the company's only consistently profitable business, had 10.3 million subscribers as of the first quarter, with revenue up about 50 per cent year-on-year, according to SpaceX's IPO filing.
The AI division, weighed down by heavy data center spending, posted steep losses last year.
Crucially, the earnings report will also trigger the first scheduled unlock of insider shares, freeing up stock that has been restricted from sale since the IPO.
That could add further pressure on the price if shareholders decide to sell.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Beaten-down Indonesian stocks feel the love as AI rally cools
Article:
SINGAPORE: As the rally in artificial intelligence (AI) stocks starts showing signs of fatigue and big investors turn sellers, some are buying back laggards and even warming to Indonesia's battered and unloved stock market.
The reprieve comes at a fragile moment for Southeast Asia's biggest economy as the still-looming threat of a downgrade from index provider MSCI has made Jakarta stocks Asia's worst-performing major stock market this year, down 28 per cent.
In contrast, shares in South Korea and Taiwan have surged due to a red-hot AI rally but recent worries about valuations and rebalancing needs have led investors to look at markets that are not as heavily tied to AI plays.
"We've been taking some profit in South Korea and buying Indonesia," said David Chao, Asia-Pacific global market strategist at Invesco in Singapore. "Indonesia is still probably the most under-looked macro growth story play."
SENTIMENT SHIFTS AS BARGAIN HUNTERS SEEK VALUE
While still tentative, sentiment is growing that the worst may be over for Indonesian markets after more than US$4 billion in foreign outflows this year amid MSCI transparency concerns and fiscal worries.
"I would say a lot of the negativity is in the price already on the equity side," said Aninda Mitra, head of Asia macro and investment strategy at BNY Investments.
Mitra said that if price multiples cheapen even more and the rupiah stabilises, despite concerns around the pending MSCI decision, "then I think there is a case to be made to start adding selectively."
Asset manager Allan Gray made its first investment in Indonesia last month, finding opportunity in Indofood Sukses Makmur, the holding company behind the country's dominant instant-noodle maker.
"On our estimates, INDF trades on just over five times earnings, which we believe is a good price to pay for a dominant, cash-generative, consumer-facing business," said Rory Kutisker-Jacobson, a portfolio manager at Allan Gray.
Banking and commodity firms are also once again popular among investors, with Citi preferring Bank Central Asia , Vale Indonesia, Alamtri Minerals and Amman Mineral International.
The Jakarta benchmark index is up more than 10 per cent so far in July while tech-heavy indexes in the region have dropped sharply, suggesting some investors are starting to rotate into lagging markets.
The rebound has come despite little improvement in the concerns that triggered this year's selloff, including questions over Indonesia's fiscal discipline and uncertainty surrounding MSCI's review.
MSCI NOVEMBER DEADLINE THE NEXT CATALYST
Indonesia has retained its emerging-markets status, with most analysts expecting MSCI to reaffirm that classification in November after extending its review to assess the impact of reforms that Jakarta regulators rolled out earlier this year.
S&P also helped calm investor nerves last week by reaffirming Indonesia's sovereign rating with a stable outlook.
Citi said Indonesia dominated client meetings in Hong Kong and Thailand earlier this month, with several investors sharing the bank's view that the stock market may have bottomed.
To be sure, not everyone is convinced that a cooling AI-driven rally will provide lasting support for Indonesian assets. Indonesia is also not the only market back in favour, with global investors revisiting Indian and Chinese equities.
The US$1.4 trillion economy has been grappling with eroding investor confidence in President Prabowo Subianto's welfare policies as fears of worsening fiscal health leave the rupiah down nearly eight per cent this year, pinned at record lows.
As a net oil importer, higher oil prices during an Iran-related supply shock have added to concerns.
"Indonesia has become a structural downgrade for many global investors," said Arthur Budaghyan, chief emerging markets and China strategist at BCA Research. "The bar for these portfolios to upgrade Indonesia and allocate more capital to it has become very high."
Even so, the retreat by foreign investors has been gradual rather than a wholesale exit.
Copley Fund Research data shows more than half of active fund managers it tracks remain overweight on Indonesia although the percentage of funds invested in the country has dropped to 80.45 per cent, a 15-year low.
"The structural case for Indonesia has not disappeared," the research firm said. "Indonesia is not a crowded trade in either direction...The cost of being wrong on a recovery is now arguably greater than the cost of further patience."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Heat and World Cup hurt UK property asking prices
Article:
LONDON: Asking prices for newly advertised British houses and apartments recorded an unusually large fall in the four weeks to July 11, as the men's soccer World Cup and record temperatures distracted buyers who also face high borrowing costs, property website Rightmove said on Monday.
Prices for property put on sale dropped by 1.0 per cent, compared with the previous month, bigger than the average 0.2 per cent fall for the period over the past 10 years.
Rightmove also said:
* Asking ⁠prices were 0.4 per cent lower than a year earlier, after a 0.5 per cent annual drop in June
* The average two-year fixed mortgage interest rate fell to 4.92 per cent from 5.07 per cent in June but was up from 4.25 per cent in February
* Sales agreed in the first half of 2026 were 6.0 per cent ⁠below ​their level a year ​earlier but were similar to volumes in the first six months of 2024
* The number of homes available for sale fell 1.0 per cent compared to a year earlier but was close to a 12-year high for the time of year
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Loke: Senawang–Port Klang rail bypass to spur Negri economy
Article:
SEREMBAN: The proposed construction of the Senawang–Port Klang bypass rail line, included in the Pakatan Harapan manifesto for the Negri Sembilan election, is expected to serve as a new catalyst for economic development in the southern part of the state.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke said the strategic project would complement the existing transportation network while strengthening connectivity between industrial areas and the port.
"When we plan to build this bypass rail line from Senawang to Port Klang, it will shorten the distance and make cargo transportation more efficient.
"This is in line with the ministry's 'road to rail' policy, where we aim to encourage freight from this area to be transported by rail.
"It also fulfils the aspirations of the Negri Sembilan government and our manifesto, which is to develop Negri Sembilan into an industrial state," he said here last night.
He said the plan would be included in the next development Rolling Plan.
"This framework has been jointly planned, and the ministry has a long-term strategy to realise the Senawang–Port Klang bypass.
"In the north, we already have the Serendah bypass, which allows freight trains from the north to avoid entering Kuala Lumpur.
"They can proceed directly via the Serendah bypass to Port Klang, but currently there is no equivalent bypass from the south," he said.
Earlier, Negri Sembilan Pakatan Harapan chairman, who is also caretaker Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun, announced the 10 key commitments in PH's manifesto for the Negri Sembilan election.
Among the announcements were five high-impact projects, including the Senawang–Port Klang bypass rail line, a new Port Dickson International Port, a Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) project, an increase in two-way Seremban–Kuala Lumpur commuter rail services, and the construction of a new hospital in Bandar Enstek, Nilai.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: South Korea's exports jump 52pct in first 20 days of July
Article:
SEOUL: South Korea's exports for the first 20 days of July jumped 52.3 per cent from a year earlier, early data showed on Tuesday.
Exports of semiconductors soared 180.6 per cent, while car exports declined 10.6 per cent, according to the government data.
Destination wise, shipments to China jumped 94.1 per cent in July from a year earlier and exports to the US increased 39.6 per cent.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US judge approves Anthropic's US$1.5bil settlement of copyright lawsuit
Article:
SAN FRANCISCO: A federal judge in San Francisco on Monday signed off on artificial intelligence company Anthropic's landmark US$1.5 billion settlement of a class action lawsuit brought by a group of authors who accused it of misusing their books to train its artificial intelligence (AI) chatbot Claude.
US District Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin granted final approval of the settlement, the largest known settlement of a US copyright case, rejecting arguments that it was too small.
The case is one of dozens brought by copyright owners including authors and news outlets against tech companies over the training of their large language models, and the first major US case to settle. Now-retired Judge William Alsup initially approved the deal last September.
"We reached this settlement in 2025, after the court's landmark ruling that training AI on books is fair use under copyright law — which remains the law today," Anthropic deputy general counsel Aparna Sridhar said in a statement.
"We are pleased that more than 91 per cent of authors and publishers covered by the settlement have claimed their share of the payment, and we're looking forward to bringing this matter to a close." The authors' lead attorney, Justin Nelson, welcomed what he called a "historic settlement."
"It is the largest known copyright recovery in history. We look forward to making distributions to the Class as promptly as possible," Nelson said in a statement, referring to payments to authors covered by the settlement.
The writers sued Anthropic in 2024, arguing that the company, which is backed by ⁠Amazon and Alphabet, used pirated versions of their books without permission to teach Claude to respond to human prompts.
Alsup ruled last June ​that Anthropic made fair ⁠use of the authors' work to train Claude, but found that the company violated their rights by saving more than seven million pirated books to a "central library" ​that would not necessarily be used for AI training.
A trial was scheduled to begin last December ⁠to determine how much Anthropic owed for the alleged piracy, with potential damages running into the hundreds of billions of dollars.
The settlement spurred objections from some authors who argued it ​was not large enough, overcompensated the plaintiffs' attorneys or wrongly excluded some copyright owners.
Judge Martinez-Olguin overruled those objections in her ruling on Monday.
The judge said that complaints about the settlement's size were "not grounded in a realistic assessment of the overall risks and rewards of a trial," and awarded the attorneys more than US$101 million of the US$187.5 million they requested in fees.
Some authors and publishers opted out of the settlement and have filed separate lawsuits against Anthropic that are still ongoing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three die after chicken lorry crashes into parked trailer
Article:
TANAH MERAH: Three people, including a 16-year-old, were killed after their lorry crashed into a parked trailer along the East-West Highway here early today.
The victims were identified as lorry driver Norazeman Abdul Aziz, 42, Mohammad Zakwan Mohd Nor, 16, and Mohd Haikal Hanifah, 29.
All three suffered serious bodily and head injuries and died at the scene in the 4am crash.
Tanah Merah Fire and Rescue operation commander Mat Nuri Mohamad said the department received an emergency call via the NG999 system at 4.26am.
He said 12 firemen from the Kemahang and Tanah Merah fire stations were deployed to the scene.
"Firemen arrived at the scene at 4.41am and found the lorry, which was transporting a supply of chickens, had crashed into a trailer parked by the roadside.
"The three victims were trapped inside the lorry. Firemen extricated them from the wreckage before Health Ministry personnel confirmed that all three had died at the scene," he said.
He added that the bodies were handed over to the police for further action.
The operation was brought under control at 5am and ended at 7.30am.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foreigner nabbed after walking naked on Klang rooftop
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A 37-year-old foreigner was arrested after he was seen walking
naked
on the roof of a house in Taman Andalas Jaya, Klang, yesterday morning.
South Klang police chief Assistant Commissioner Lim Jit Huey said members of the public spotted the suspect at 7am before calling the Fire and Rescue Department to help lower him safely.
The suspect was subsequently detained to assist in investigations and was found to have no valid identification documents.
"The South Klang Criminal Investigation Department has opened an investigation paper under Section 427 of the Penal Code for committing mischief and Section 377D of the Penal Code for gross indecency.
"The man is also being investigated under Section 6(1)(c) of the Immigration Act 1959/63 for failing to possess valid travel documents," he said in a statement.
Lim said Section 427 of the Penal Code carries a jail term of up to five years, a fine, or both, while Section 377D provides for a maximum sentence of two years' imprisonment.
Offences under Section 6(1)(c) of the Immigration Act 1959/63 carry a fine, up to five years in prison, or both.
"Police also advise the public not to share videos of the incident on social media to avoid causing alarm among the community," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unions back PM's promotion review, seek focus on merit and enforcement incentives
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Civil service unions have welcomed the prime minister's directive to review promotion processes, while calling for an emphasis on merit and specialised incentives over seniority.
National Union of the Teaching Profession (NUTP) secretary-general Fouzi Singon said the union supports the move to streamline career progression, particularly by further empowering the "Excellent Teacher" incentive.
He said it allows deserving teachers to bypass the usual eight-year wait altogether.
"This existing fast-track allows high-performing teachers to be promoted in as little as three years.
"It is a vital avenue that allows specialists to reach senior Jusa grades while remaining in the classroom, much like a medical specialist in a hospital," he told the New Straits Times.
He said there was a new and unique trend in the teaching profession, where professionals from sectors such as engineering, banking, and medicine are now joining the service.
"In recent interviews, many of the applicants were engineers, and we've also had people from banking and even doctors trying to join the system," he said.
However, he cautioned that early attrition remains a challenge, with teachers in their 50s retiring early due to health issues or the difficulty of keeping up with new technology.
He said NUTP has not seen evidence of young teachers quitting over slow promotions or pay concerns.
"Young teachers don't really leave the profession unless they go into business. Jumping from teaching to another career altogether, that has never really happened," he said.
To further improve the service, NUTP has proposed that vacancies for administrative posts be openly advertised for transparency and called for additional incentives for young talent serving as lecturers at teacher training institutes (IPG).
"IPG lecturers currently receive the same pay as classroom teachers despite the added responsibility of supervising practical training. We are proposing incentives to attract talent into these positions, especially in remote areas," he said.
Fouzi was commenting Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's announcement that he had directed a review of the
processes for promotion
among civil servants, shortening the waiting period for career advancement.
He had said the waiting period of eight years was too long a time.
Meanwhile, the Immigration Services Union of Peninsular Malaysia (KPISM) said any review of the promotion structure must holistically account for the high-risk nature of enforcement work.
Its president, Ajeet Singh Gurbax Singh, said the review should factor in workload, task complexity, and the heavy responsibilities borne by officers at entry points and detention depots.
"Improvements to the promotion structure, allowances, and incentives will strengthen motivation and productivity," he said.
The union also renewed its call for a long-standing RM200 monthly enforcement incentive proposal.
"Our officers on the ground have been waiting long enough, we hope this review will finally include a resolution on this," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Saudi condemns Houthi maritime blockade threat
Article:
RIYADH: Saudi Arabia on Monday condemned what it described as allegations by Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis that the kingdom was besieging the Yemeni people, after the group announced it would impose a maritime blockade on Saudi Arabia.
The Houthis said on Monday they would impose a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, opening a potential new front against the United States in its war with Iran and raising the threat to global energy supplies and trade beyond the Gulf.
In their statement, the Houthis' armed forces said they were declaring "a maritime embargo against the criminal Saudi enemy, based on the equation of 'an eye for an eye', effective immediately" in response to what they called "an unjust and oppressive siege" imposed on Yemen by the Saudis.
The Saudi foreign ministry condemned allegations by the Houthi military spokesperson that the kingdom was besieging Yemen and imposing a maritime blockade. Saudi would take  "all necessary measures" to protect its ships, the ministry said in a statement.
Yemen has been mired in civil war for more than a decade since the Houthis seized the capital, Sanaa, prompting a Saudi-led military intervention in 2015 in support of the internationally recognised government.
The conflict has since evolved into one of the world's worst humanitarian crises, leaving the country divided between a Saudi-backed government in Aden and a Houthi administration in Sanaa.
Cargo ships and other vessels have regularly arrived at the port of Houthi-controlled Hodeidah, including in recent weeks, delivering food fuel and other goods, according to shipping data.--REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New UK PM Burnham stamps authority with cabinet shakeup
Article:
LONDON: New UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham took power Monday, purging the cabinet of his predecessor's loyalists as he promised a 10-year plan to ease cost-of-living pressures and signalled a tilt to the left.
He immediately got to work after replacing Keir Starmer in Downing Street, holding separate calls with US President Donald Trump and Ukrainian leader Volodymyr Zelenskyy before appointing his ministerial team.
Burnham, 56, pledged to re-industrialise Britain and decentralise power as well as end sleeping rough on the streets after he met King Charles III at Buckingham Palace to become the head of government following Starmer's departure.
The monarch's fourth prime minister since he acceded to the throne in 2022, and Britain's seventh in just over a decade, Burnham said the country needed to show the world it could "regain stability."
Accompanied by his wife Marie-France van Heel, Burnham was greeted in Downing Street by resounding applause from supporters, including his mother.
Standing outside his new official residence at Number 10, he vowed to create "a new political model" and "build a new economy."
In his first call with Trump, Burnham underlined his "commitment to defence and security", a UK government spokesperson said.
Trump said he had a "very good conversation" with Burnham and that the two leaders would meet "in the not too distant future."
Burnham "has got a big job ahead of him, but he will be able to do it and, of course, the U.S.A. will be there to help!" Trump posted on Truth Social, noting they discussed trade, military cooperation and demining the Strait of Hormuz.
To Zelenskyy, Burnham reaffirmed Britain's "resolute commitment" to Ukraine in its defence of Russia's invasion, according to Downing Street.
He also moved swiftly to stamp his authority on the new government by sacking several Starmer loyalists from his cabinet, including Rachel Reeves and David Lammy, while moving some ministers to other jobs and promoting allies.
- 'STRONGER AND FAIRER' -
Ex-defence secretary John Healey replaced Reeves as finance minister, Ed Miliband became foreign minister and left-wing figurehead Angela Rayner returned to government.
Wes Streeting, who abandoned plans to run against Burnham for the Labour leadership, is now defence minister.
Shabana Mahmood was reappointed interior minister to see through her hardline reforms of Britain's asylum system.
In his brief speech, Burnham said he would "bring forward a new plan for Britain, a 10-year plan", without giving specifics.
Starmer had similarly talked of his intention to lead a "decade of national renewal" before Labour MPs ditched him last month in favour of Burnham, who only returned to parliament four weeks ago.
Burnham promised to "give people some breathing space now, some help with the cost of living", which he said he would start to announce Tuesday.
He also said his first instruction would be a pledge to "end rough sleeping", a measure he attempted to implement as a regional mayor in northern England, although it was not fully successful.
Other announcements included a vague promise to re-industrialise Britain through public procurement and to build more public housing.
He repeated his belief that "in the 1980s Britain took some wrong turns", as he pledged to decentralise power away from Westminster and "put life's essentials back under stronger public control to make them affordable."
Starmer departed Downing Street with his wife earlier, insisting his two years in office had made Britain "stronger and fairer."
- 'MOMENTUM' -
He leaves multiple challenges for Burnham, including a tepid economy, high government borrowing costs, a ballooning welfare bill and irregular migrants arriving in small boats that have fuelled support for the hard-right Reform UK party.
Unpredictable energy prices due to the US-Iran war and volatile relations with Washington under Trump also threaten to buffet his premiership.
Burnham, an MP between 2001 and 2017, has little room to manoeuvre amid high public debt and strict financial rules requiring him to balance government spending against tax revenue.
Starmer led Labour to a landslide election victory in July 2024 after 14 years of Conservative rule, but his premiership became characterised by scandals, missteps and policy U-turns.
Burnham, the ex-mayor of Greater Manchester nicknamed the "King of the North", was parachuted in by Labour after Starmer resigned, with MPs viewing him as the party's best chance of reining in Reform, led by Nigel Farage.
But his swift "coronation" without a contest as Labour leader and now prime minister, by virtue of Labour's huge parliamentary majority, has raised questions about how he will achieve his aims.
Opposition politicians have questioned his mandate and called for a snap election.
French President Emmanuel Macron sent congratulations, urging Burnham to "give new momentum to the partnership" with the European Union. --AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Human smuggling cases up 18pc in Kedah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kedah police have recorded an 18.52 per cent increase in arrests linked to human smuggling along the Malaysia-Thailand border between January and June this year, compared with the same period last year, reflecting sustained cross-border criminal activity in the northern frontier.
State police chief Datuk Adzli Abu Shah said 256 individuals were detained in the first six months of 2026, up from 216 arrests recorded during the corresponding period in 2025.
He said of those detained this year, 30 were identified as transporters while the remaining 226 were migrants.
"For human smuggling cases, in 2025, police recorded 216 arrests involving 36 transporters and 180 migrants," he said.
"This year, 256 individuals were arrested, comprising 30 transporters and 226 migrants. This represents an increase of 18.52 per cent."
Adzli said the figures showed the continued presence of organised smuggling networks operating along the border, particularly involving the movement of undocumented migrants through established transit routes.
He also said enforcement efforts have led to arrests in drug trafficking cases within the same border areas.
For the period January to June 2025, Kedah police recorded 11 arrests involving drug distribution cases.
"For the same period this year, 11 individuals were arrested involving 10 cases," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fuel smugglers exploit residents' unused subsidy quotas in Perlis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Syndicates involved in subsidised fuel smuggling in Perlis have shifted tactics to exploit the identity cards of legitimate users who do not fully utilise their fuel quota.
Perlis Internal Security and Public Order Department chief Assistant Commissioner Gun Kan Seng said the method represents a departure from earlier practices that relied heavily on modified vehicles and altered fuel tanks to obtain subsidised fuel in bulk.
"We believe some syndicates target users who use only a small portion of their quota, such as motorcyclists, before exploiting the remaining allocation.
"Intelligence also indicates that syndicates may be using the identity cards of such users to maximise repeated refuelling transactions at petrol stations, allowing fuel to be accumulated over time before being consolidated at illegal storage sites," he said.
He added that this shift in tactics appears to be an attempt to avoid detection by dispersing transactions across multiple legitimate users rather than relying on suspicious vehicles or bulk refuelling patterns.
"Fuel is usually accumulated through repeated refuelling before being consolidated at illegal storage sites for further distribution and smuggling across the border," he said.
Gun said since the launch of Op Tiris 4.0, authorities in Perlis have recorded 14 cases involving subsidised fuel smuggling, with total seizures and estimated value amounting to RM455,375.
He said the cases involved coordinated enforcement actions by his department, district police headquarters, the marine police and other units.
Of the total, he said three cases involved raids on illegal fuel storage sites used by syndicates to accumulate fuel before distribution.
[[nid:1493726]]
"Two illegal storage sites were raided in the Padang Besar area, while another was located in Kuala Perlis near the coastal area," he said, adding that these operations had effectively disrupted such activities, with no further active storage detected for now.
Gun said authorities were continuing intelligence and monitoring efforts to prevent attempts to revive illegal storage operations, particularly along known smuggling routes and border crossings.
He  said  seven police reports were lodged involving missing fuel quotas, with investigations still ongoing.
He added that there were 42 petrol stations in Perlis, of which six have been identified as hotspots and another four as potential hotspots for subsidy leakage.
He said enforcement has been intensified at these locations, with the General Operations Force (GOF) deployed on static duty at hotspot stations while task force units conduct regular patrols at other identified sites.
"About 30 GOF personnel have been deployed at hotspot stations, while around 40 task force members from state and district levels are conducting daily monitoring at potential hotspots," he said.
Gun said enforcement coordination also involved multiple agencies, including the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry, Immigration Department, Customs Department, armed forces, Road Transport Department and other agencies to disrupt smuggling.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Syndicates tap locals with 'clean profiles' to move contraband
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Cross-border smuggling syndicates are exploiting  border residents by recruiting Malaysians to play key operational roles — ranging from transporters and lookouts to logistics facilitators — in a bid to reduce the risk of detection along Malaysia's northern border.
Kedah police chief Datuk Adzli Abu Shah said the involvement of these folk has become a key feature in the latest smuggling networks, with syndicates deliberately leveraging Malaysians' "clean profiles" and access to locally registered vehicles to move goods and migrants discreetly.
He said those living along the border were frequently hired to transport contraband or migrants to transit houses, where their less suspicious backgrounds allow them to bypass enforcement scrutiny.
"The border folk are often hired to transport goods or migrants from the border to transit houses because they have clean profiles and locally registered vehicles that are less likely to raise suspicion."
Adzli said some border folk have also been identified acting as "tonto", or lookouts, tracking the movements of enforcement agencies and relaying real-time information through messaging platforms such as WhatsApp and Telegram.
He said others were involved as logistics facilitators, providing transit storage facilities and vehicles registered under third-party names through purchase or rental arrangements.
"This network operates in a structured manner involving both countries. These syndicates do not operate alone," he said.
"Each member has a specific role such as planner, supplier, transporter, storage handler and receiver."
He added that other dominant cross-border crimes along the Malaysia-Thailand border include drug trafficking and arms smuggling, which remain key enforcement priorities for security agencies operating in the area.
Meanwhile, Internal Security and Public Order Department (General Operations Force) deputy director Datuk Mohamad Suzrin Mohamad Rodhi said smuggling syndicates had also adopted layered operating structures involving "tekong darat", runners, reconnaissance teams and  informants to minimise the risk of arrest.
He said there has also been increasing evidence of  participation of border folk, with some individuals misusing their freedom of movement to act as "tekong darat" and runners.
"This includes individuals acting as spotters and informants who report every movement of security forces along the border," he said.
For migrant smuggling activities, he said undocumented migrants were typically placed temporarily in transit houses, lodging facilities or concealed areas before being moved to designated destinations.
He added that communication within syndicates was largely conducted through social media platforms and messaging applications to coordinate movements.
"There are also elements of human trafficking in these operations involving travel debt, salary deductions and control over victims' movements."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: N. Korea reaffirms support for Moscow's 'Ukrainian dispute'
Article:
SEOUL: North Korea reaffirmed its support for Moscow's war on Ukraine as the two foreign ministers held talks in Moscow, Pyongyang's state media reported Tuesday.
Choe Son Hui and Sergey Lavrov held a "third strategic dialogue" on Monday, at a time when the partnership between the two countries is "vigorously developing," said a press statement published by Korean Central News Agency (KCNA).
"The DPRK side expressed invariable support for all the internal and external policies of the Russian Federation to eliminate the root cause of the Ukrainian dispute and defend its national sovereignty, territorial integrity and international justice," the statement said.
Russian President Vladimir Putin met the visiting diplomat on Sunday to thank North Korea for its support, which has included soldiers.
"I would like once again to express my gratitude for the support provided to the special military operation we are conducting," Putin said, according to a video released by the Kremlin.
"We will never forget" the "heroic deeds" of North Korean soldiers, who would be "honoured in the same way as our compatriots", he added.
North Korea has sent troops to Russia's Kursk region and supplied weapons systems to support Moscow's war effort.
In return, analysts say the impoverished and isolated country has received financial assistance, food, energy supplies and military technology from Moscow.
During a visit to Pyongyang by senior Russian officials in late April, Kim reaffirmed his support for Russia and pledged to help Moscow achieve victory in what he called its "sacred war."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Azam denies role in RM178mil deal won by brother's firm [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:   Former Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki has distanced himself from the RM178.29 million contract awarded to a company linked to his brother, saying he has no knowledge of the matter.
Responding to an article published by
The Edge Malaysia
, Azam said his brother's business affairs had nothing to do with him.
It was reported that Integriti Padu Sdn Bhd had secured a six-year contract to develop and operate the Road Charge and Vehicle Entry Permit (RC/VEP) system at the Malaysia-Singapore border.
"I don't know anything about this. It is his business.
"So does this mean whoever is associated with me is going to be questioned?
"I have retired and it is up to the Transport Ministry to award the project to his company," he told the
New Straits Times
.
Azam, who retired as MACC chief commissioner on May 12, also defended his family, saying they should not come under scrutiny solely because of their relationship with him.
"My family's DNA is all good. I have a sister who is a well-known ear, nose and throat specialist in this country.
"Does this mean she will also be put under the spotlight? Whoever wants to be safe, please don't get close to me," he said.
According to
The Edge
, the Transport Ministry awarded the RM178.29 million contract to Integriti Padu on May 26 following a request for proposals issued in June last year.
The report said Integriti Padu's executive chairman is Datuk Nasir Baki, Azam's brother.
The company is tasked with developing, operating and maintaining the RC/VEP system for six years.
Azam's tenure as MACC chief commissioner was previously overshadowed by a controversy over his shareholdings after allegations surfaced in late 2021 that he held millions of shares and warrants in several public-listed companies while serving as a senior civil servant.
In January 2022, Azam denied any wrongdoing, saying the shares had been purchased by his brother using his trading account.
Earlier this year, Azam again came under scrutiny following Bloomberg reports alleging irregularities involving his shareholdings in a public-listed company while he was MACC chief.
He has since filed a RM100 million defamation suit against Bloomberg over the reports.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Semporna initiative nets 15 tonnes of marine waste
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
MORE than 15 tonnes of floating solid waste have been removed from Semporna waters through the Puspabumi Eco Pledge Programme.
Reef Check Malaysia senior programme manager Adzmin Fatta said the initiative was launched following reports of rubbish-filled plastic bags drifting in open waters.
"Last year, we received reports of plastic bags containing waste floating in our waters, believed to have been thrown from boats operated by tourism operators.
"Rather than waiting for enforcement alone, Puspabumi (Persatuan Usahawan Pelancongan Bumiputera Semporna) members set up a monitoring system to ensure waste generated from tourism activities is brought back to the mainland and disposed of responsibly," he said in a statement.
The programme, launched last November, is a joint initiative between Reef Check Malaysia and Puspabumi to promote sustainable practices among local tourism operators.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump orders 50pct tariff on many Canadian goods
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump signed orders Monday to impose new 50-per cent tariffs on many Canadian goods, claiming "discriminatory treatment" by Ottawa against American alcohol, automobile and dairy products.
The tariffs will take effect in 30 days and cover a range of items including wine, hockey sticks and cement, according to a White House fact sheet.
Trump, who saw many of his tariffs struck down by the Supreme Court earlier this year, tapped an untested legal provision for the new duties – Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930.
The latest duties will not apply to energy, potash and goods already impacted by sector-specific tariffs, the White House said.
Crucially, however, they will hit products covered under the US-Mexico-Canada free trade agreement (USMCA).
While Trump has slapped sweeping duties on US trading partners since returning to the presidency last year, the orders have generally exempted goods entering his country under the North American free trade pact.
His latest actions threaten to further strain ties between Washington and Ottawa, and come just days after he threatened Canada with increased tariffs over wildfire smoke that drifted into the United States.
The White House, in announcing the new tariffs, said Canada was one of only two countries – along with China – to retaliate against Trump's sweeping tariffs last year.
It also took aim at the fact that most Canadian provinces have halted purchases of US alcohol, boycotting the products over Trump's tariff threats and repeated calls for annexation of Canada as America's "51st state."
"Canada has taken US alcohol products off Canadian shelves, given better market access to dairy products from the European Union, and has put a cap on US vehicle exports to Canada from companies reshoring to the United States," charged US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer in a statement.
The tariff announcement aims to "hold Canada accountable for its retaliation and discrimination," he added.
- 'LITIGATION RISK' -
Trump's move marks the first time that Section 338 has been used to impose tariffs, Scott Lincicome of the libertarian Cato Institute told AFP.
Many experts believe that the law has been superceded by other authorities, he said, arguing that Trump has "demonstrated a willingness to use and abuse any statute on the books."
Ryan Majerus, a former US trade official, told AFP that the law is "subject to a lot of litigation risk" and has never been tested.
"This is clearly designed to get some leverage over Canada," added Majerus, who is now a partner at law firm King & Spalding.
He noted that negotiations surrounding the USMCA were ongoing, and that the United States' talks with Mexico have proceeded at a much faster pace than those with Canada.
"This could be an effort to try to get them going," he said, pointing to the 30-day delay in the tariffs' implementation.
If the tariffs were to take effect as announced, however, Majerus expects that the removal of exemptions for USMCA imports would be highly negative to US supply chains and businesses.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RTM plans to air more global sports events
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
RADIO Televisyen Malaysia (RTM) will announce the acquisition of broadcasting rights for more global sporting events after its successful free coverage of the 2026 World Cup, said Datuk Seri Fahmi Fadzil.
The communications minister said broadcasting director-general Ashwad Ismail had informed him that RTM would be airing more tournaments.
"That will be announced later," he said after attending the Unity Merdeka x RTM World Cup 2026 programme at Dataran Merdeka here early yesterday.
He added that the ministry would await the full viewership figures before assessing the broadcast performance.
Fahmi said although there were some technical issues at the start of the World Cup coverage, those were successfully resolved.
"... I feel that this is also a good opportunity for us to explore new technologies, including the ability to watch television on smartphones," he added.
Meanwhile, Ashwad said his department would continue to strengthen strategic partnerships with media organisations to ensure the public had access to quality content, including major sporting events.
He said the aim was to unite media practitioners around providing the best content to the people, rather than competing over broadcasting rights.
"This is not about who gets the exclusive rights or who doesn't, and not about who wins or who loses.
"When it comes to something that Malaysians need, the department believes that it goes beyond competitive rivalry."
Ashwad added that this collaborative approach would extend beyond the World Cup to future major events. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang Port key logistics gateway to Thai border
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
JELEBU: Penang Port continues to play an important role as a logistics gateway for southern Thailand by handling container and cargo movements between the neighbouring country and Malaysia, said Transport Minister Anthony Loke.
He said the port is handling a large number of containers to and from southern Thailand, thus strengthening its role as a regional logistics hub.
"We do handle a lot of containers to and from Thailand, so Penang Port is now a gateway to southern Thailand.
"The cargo is transported by containers via train from southern Thailand to Penang Port and vice versa," he said after meeting residents in Pekan Titi here yesterday.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim recently asked the Transport Ministry to discuss the use of ports on the Malaysia-Thailand border as well as in Penang to strengthen the development of infrastructure and logistics networks.
Anwar said transportation were among the matters discussed with his Thai counterpart Anutin Charnvirakul during the leader's recent official visit to Malaysia.
Meanwhile, Loke said the effort was not new because it was being actively implemented, including the development of the Perlis Inland Port (PIP) as a support centre for logistics operations.
"PIP is being developed as a place for storage, warehousing and container storage." — Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Ukraine fired 400 drones at Moscow'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
MOST of the over 400 drones that targeted the Moscow region overnight were intercepted, but at least two people were wounded, said Russian authorities yesterday.
Kyiv has in recent months intensified its strikes on Russian territory, describing them as retribution for more than four years of bombardments against its territory.
"From 8.30pm until 5am, more than 400 enemy drones were flying in the direction of Moscow region. Most were neutralised by air defence forces at distant approaches; 85 UAVs (unmanned aerial vehicles) were destroyed on approach to Moscow," wrote mayor Sergei Sobyanin on the state-backed MAX platform.
Capital region governor Andrey Vorobyov reported damage in Podolsk, Domodedovo and Odintsovo, saying private houses and civilian infrastructure were hit.
"According to preliminary information, one person was wounded in Domodedovo as a result of a fire at a private house and another on the M-4 highway, where a drone fell near a vehicle," said Vorobyov.
Domodedovo Hospital also reported yesterday that six people, including three foreigners, were hospitalised with blast wounds in the Moscow region.
It was unclear if those injuries were the same ones described by the governor and whether they were linked to the drone attacks.
Domodedovo is to the south of Moscow and home to an international airport serving the Russian capital.
On Sunday, Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said "Russia carried out one of its largest ballistic missile attacks on Kyiv" the night before, which killed one person and wounded 16.
Meanwhile, Ukraine said Russian missile strikes on a Turkish-owned cargo ship carrying grain had killed six people, hours after Moscow fired a fresh barrage of missiles at Kyiv.
Ukraine's navy said Russia had launched three cruise missiles at the 'Golden Leo', which flies the flag of Guinea-Bissau, as it was "leaving the combat zone with a cargo of grain".
Foreign Minister Andriy Sybiga said the crew had included citizens of Syria and India, while Prime Minister Sergiy Koretsky said the vessel was struck near Odesa after leaving port.
"Eight crew members were rescued. Two of them were injured. Unfortunately, as of now, six sailors are confirmed dead and four more are missing," said Transport Minister Mykola Kalashnyk on Telegram.  — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fahmi: PH has no shortage of Malay-Muslim leaders
Article:
KUALA PILAH: Pakatan Harapan (PH) communications director Datuk Seri Fahmi Fadzil has dismissed claims that the federal government is neglecting Malay sensitivities.
Responding to former Umno Youth chief Khairy Jamaluddin's assertion that Barisan Nasional's (BN) cooperation with Perikatan Nasional (PN) in the Negri Sembilan election was intended to signal the need for PH to pay greater attention to Malay concerns, Fahmi said Khairy was not the only person who understood such issues.
"As a Malay, I am very aware of and sensitive to the various issues being raised. My late grandfather and grandmother were Umno members.
"My late grandmother was a staunch Umno supporter who fought for the interest of Malays and Muslims.
"How could I ever abandon those values?" he told reporters after attending the Ziarah Kasih programme at Felda Palong 2, Jempol, yesterday.
Fahmi said Khairy's continued emphasis on Malay and Islamic issues appeared to be aimed at rebuilding his political standing following his return to Umno.
"He was out of Umno for quite some time. Now that he's rebuilding his support base, he'll say whatever he thinks is necessary to make sure people notice him," he said.
The Communications Minister also rejected claims that PH lacked Malay-Muslim leaders capable of defending the interests of the Malay community and Islam.
"Is Anwar Ibrahim not a Malay-Muslim? Is Mohamad Sabu not a Malay-Muslim? Is Dzulkefly Ahmad not a Malay-Muslim? Are Amirudin Shari and Aminuddin Harun not Malay-Muslims?
"Let's stop spreading falsehoods. Remember, as Muslims, if we speak falsely, we will be answerable in the hereafter.
"My advice to Khairy is: don't go overboard. Don't become so fixated on certain views that you end up denying the presence of Malay-Muslim leaders in PH and reinforcing false perceptions about the coalition."
Yesterday, Khairy said BN's decision to work with PN in the Negri Sembilan state election was intended to send a message to PH.
He claimed some Umno members believed the federal government was not giving sufficient attention to Malay sensitivities and had failed to deliver on several of its promises.
Khairy also alleged that PH frequently used Umno's presence in the unity government as an excuse for not implementing its reform agenda.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Talks start on UN treaty to protect the elderly as life expectancy rises
Article:
A UNITED Nations meeting last week heard calls for an end to ageism and better protection against what campaigners say are hidden abuses as negotiations began on a treaty to strengthen the rights of older people.
The week of talks in Geneva that ended last Friday were initiated and chaired by Argentina in efforts to combat exclusion, discrimination and neglect as life expectancy rises.
The UN projects that the number of people over 65 will double in the next 50 years to become a fifth of the world's population.
"Our objective is not just to address the needs of the present, but also to prepare a system that can meet the needs of the future," said Carlos Mario Foradori, Argentina's ambassador to the UN in Geneva.
"This goal is to build an instrument that strengthens the dignity, protection and the rights of millions of older persons globally."
Brazil, Slovenia, the Philippines and Gambia are the proposed treaty's other main backers, and Chile and South Africa were among countries that voiced support in the week-long talks.
Negotiators are set to meet again in Geneva in October and it is not clear how long the negotiations might last. It can take years for such treaties to be agreed on. Although there are already human rights treaties that have non-discrimination clauses based on race and gender, none exist for age.
"There are many situations where people are not fully protected by existing law," said Heidrun Mollenkopf, president of AGE Platform Europe, a network of older people.
"It's completely hidden what's going on," she said, referring to what she said were abuses in nursing homes by carers that have included chemical restraints to control the behaviour of people with dementia.
There had even been cases of homicide, she said, without identifying such incidents.
A UN-appointed independent expert on the human rights of older people recommended reforms in a 2021 report, saying ageism was pervasive globally and that deeply embedded stereotypes about age were informing laws and policies.
Rights groups refer to examples such as mandatory retirement ages, age limits on jury duty and diminished cancer screening offers for people above a certain age.
"There are age restrictions that go completely unchallenged," said Bridget Sleap, a senior researcher at Human Rights Watch, a rights watchdog.
"The idea that people should just stop working because they have a birthday, it's arbitrary."
Informal UN meetings on addressing discrimination against older people have taken place since 2011 but campaigners say the high proportion of Covid-19 pandemic deaths among the aged increased the momentum for formal negotiations.
Successive heatwaves in Europe that have disproportionately killed seniors and prompted some governments to provide support have also increased the pressure, say campaigners.
Margaret Gillis, 67, founding President of the International Longevity Centre Canada, said she expects a "fight".
She said autocratic states could try to water down a treaty, and other states might resist if they feared new healthcare costs.
"I hope a treaty will come in my lifetime," said Mollenkopf, who is 85. "But I'm afraid it might not."
The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Khalil al-Hayya: Gaza negotiator who will now lead Hamas
Article:
FOR months, veteran Hamas leader Khalil al-Hayya spearheaded the group's indirect ceasefire negotiations with arch-foe Israel in an effort to halt the war in Gaza.
On Monday, the Palestinian Islamist movement named him as its new chief, favouring him over longtime leader Khaled Meshaal.
"Khalil al-Hayya was elected by an overwhelming majority," a senior Hamas official told AFP, adding that he is expected to formally assume the role next week.
"The election process was conducted through a highly complex and secretive mechanism because of the difficult security situation and the occupation's assassination of several political and military leaders," another source in Hamas said.
A former official from the Muslim Brotherhood and one of the founders of Hamas, Hayya is viewed as a moderate, and will replace slain leader Yahya Sinwar.
Along with Meshaal, he survived an Israeli assassination attempt in Doha in September 2025 that left several dead, including one of his sons and his chief of staff.
The Qatari capital serves as an informal base for Hamas, from which it can cultivate ties with the Arab and Islamic world.
From Qatar and Turkiye, the tall and bearded Hayya will lead Hamas, including in Gaza, where the movement still controls large swathes of the territory along with a security apparatus comprising thousands of fighters.
Hayya is known to have good relations with Lebanon's Hizbollah, with Arab countries such as Qatar, Egypt and Algeria, as well as with Iran, his movement's main financial and military backer, a Hamas official close to him told AFP.
The official highlighted Hayya's pragmatic and conversative temperament.
"He doesn't get carried away easily, and is respected by all members of the political bureau as well as by military commanders," the official said.
Hayya was raised in a religious and conservative family in Gaza, before studying at the territory's Islamic University.
He then completed a master's degree in Jordan, followed by a doctorate in Islamic law in Sudan.
Hayya spent three years in Israeli prisons in the late 1990s and survived two other assassination attempts, in 2007 and in 2014.
Hayya, who has repeatedly stressed the importance of armed struggle, lost several family members in Israeli military operations, including one that targeted his house in the northern Gaza Strip in 2007, leaving his wife and three of his children dead.
In 2006, Hayya headed Hamas's parliamentary bloc, which had just won elections.
The subsequent months of political and administrative discord marked the beginning of the schism between the Islamist movement and Fatah, the party of Palestinian president Mahmud Abbas.
Hayya's predecessor Sinwar, whom Israel accused of being one of the principal architects of the October 7, 2023 attack, was killed by Israeli forces in Gaza a year later.
Since his death, Hamas has been led by a five-member leadership council headed by senior official Mohammed Darwish.
A senior Hamas official told AFP that the council would be dissolved, with its members submitting their resignations to the group's Shura Council.
"The political bureau will meet in Istanbul within days to form committees and begin restructuring Hamas's leadership bodies," the official said.
* The article is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lebanon museum depicts destruction of homes
Article:
TEARS streamed down south Lebanon resident Fatima Hajj Ali's face as she stared at a host of keys hanging like windchimes from the ceiling of a Beirut museum — each one symbolising a home, like her own, destroyed by Israel.
Hajj Ali is among the thousands of southerners who lost their houses in the recent conflicts between Israel and Hizbollah, the first of which broke out in 2023 when the group launched attacks in support of its ally Hamas, and the second in March when it entered the Middle East war on the side of its backer Iran.
"We were supposed to go home and open the door with the key, but there is no door any more," said the 23-year-old.
Despite a lull in fighting following the signing of a memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran on June 17, intermittent Israeli strikes continue, as do widespread demolitions in and around occupied villages, making it impossible for many people to return.
The exhibition "Hkeeli ya Jnoub", or "Tell me, O South", features pictures and videos preserving the memory of southern Lebanon at the capital's Beit Beirut museum.
Walking through, Hajj Ali reminisced on her home in Nabatieh al-Fawqa, which she was only able to visit once after an April truce that ultimately failed to stop the fighting.
"Half the house collapsed and half remained," she said. "I long for sunset and to hear the call to prayer in our garden while I drink my coffee," said the psychologist, adding that Beirut had "beautiful" places, but "they are not home".
One of the projects on display is "Keys Without Homes", which comprises videos of three southerners who kept the keys to their houses, even though they no longer exist.
The artist, 36-year-old Adeeb Farhat, himself from the south, said the idea came to him during the previous war in 2024, when he feared losing his own home.
"I was constantly haunted by the question: What will happen to my house? Will it be bombed? And how will my relationship with my house key change? Will we become the new Palestinians?" he said.
There is a longstanding tradition among Palestinians of keeping the keys of homes they lost during the Nakba — or "catastrophe" in Arabic — which saw the flight and expulsion of an estimated 760,000 Palestinian Arabs during the creation of the State of Israel in 1948.
Within the exhibition halls, a bedroom, living room and kitchen — complete with a glass jug, coffee pot, and spice containers — recreate details of daily life in the homes of southern Lebanese residents.
The exhibition also includes an old photograph of the coastal city of Tyre, a black-and-white video of Nabatieh, and notebooks in which visitors wrote down their memories of the south.
In another work called "What Remains", Sama Beydoun, 29, and living in Paris, showed pictures of her grandfather's now-destroyed home in Bint Jbeil, near the border with Israel, which she last saw in 2025.
However, a technical glitch resulted in most of the images appearing blurry, making them look like a "dream", said Beydoun.
"I remember how many people this house brought together, how my family grew up there, how many generations it witnessed, and how life changed, while some things remained constant, like the weekly Sunday gatherings," she said.
"Life was very simple, but it was beautiful."
In a photo essay called "Manufacturing Estrangements", Rawan Mazeh, 29, tells the story of a couple detained in the notorious Khiam Prison, run by the South Lebanon Army, an Israeli proxy militia, during Israel's 22-year occupation of south Lebanon that ended in 2000.
To Mazeh, the exhibition "created a comfortable place where people could come and feel close to their land".
The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hamas names Khalil Al-Hayya as new overall leader
Article:
CAIRO: Hamas has named Khalil Al-Hayya as its overall leader, according to a statement from the Palestinian resistance group on Monday, in a move an analyst says could signal a tougher stance by the group towards disarmament calls.
Hayya replaces Yahya Sinwar, who was killed in combat with Israeli forces in October 2024 as Hamas fought a full-scale, two-year war with Israel in the Gaza Strip.
Hayya, the group's exiled Gaza chief and top negotiator, has become an increasingly central figure in the leadership of Hamas since the deaths of Sinwar and Ismail Haniyeh, who was killed in Iran in July 2024.
He is seen as more hardline than Khaled Meshaal, the head of the group's office in exile and the other frontrunner for the leadership.
Hayya was among top Hamas officials targeted in 2025 in an Israeli attack in Doha, where he is based, Israeli officials told Reuters.
The group faces some of the toughest challenges since it was founded in 1987, battered by two years of war ignited by its Oct 7, 2023, attack on Israel and facing international demands to disarm.
While fighting has abated in Gaza since the US-brokered ceasefire in October, Israel still holds more than 60 per cent of the coastal enclave, attacks continue, and conditions for Gaza's 2 million people remain dire.
On Monday, an Israeli strike killed at least three Palestinians driving in a vehicle in Khan Younis in southern Gaza, medics said.
The deaths add to a toll of more than 1,150 Palestinians, mostly civilians, killed by Israeli attacks since the October ceasefire, according to Gaza health officials.
Four Israeli soldiers were killed by fighters in Gaza over the same period. Hamas does not usually disclose its losses.
Since Sinwar was killed, Hamas has resorted to a five-member higher council that included Hayya and Meshaal, in addition to three other officials.
The election of a Hamas chief is a lengthy process that began in January and was interrupted by the wars in Iran and Lebanon and continuing Israeli military operations in Gaza.
The selection is made by a 50-member council made up of Hamas members in Gaza, the occupied West Bank — including Israeli prisons — and those in exile.
